records.summary: ApplicationTopologyBuilder is the mutable authoring-side assembler for app-level topology files, components, styles, services, registrations, and routes.
records.rationale: The authoring API needs a topology model that can express intended app structure before source exists, while recognition and verification later prove what the app actually means. This builder is the topology-construction boundary; recipes should name intent and preferences rather than rebuilding topology graphs by hand.
guidance: Start here when authoring operations need to assemble entrypoints, components, templates, styles, services, registrations, routes, or component dependencies.
Keep generator taste, formatting policy, and recipe-specific preferences out of application topology.

When recipe requests allow custom paths, derive topology component references and template/style import specifiers from the same paths used by source plans; `ApplicationTopologyBuilder.component(...)` owns component reference specifier derivation through `referenceFromPath`, and default-layout specifiers such as `./app` should not leak into custom-path topology.

Large recipe topology builders should stay as short phase assemblers plus named topology artifact helpers, such as adding a root component, form component, state service, route, or entrypoint. That gives Atlas stable handles for topology intent without pushing recipe taste into `ApplicationTopologyBuilder`.

This frontier is nextActionPolicy=when-touched: use it as topology grounding when app-building, fixture, or authoring-operation work needs topology assembly, not as default autonomous work just because the builder is live.
`ApplicationTopology` and reopened `AppTopology` share the same style asset vocabulary: owner kind, asset kind, source kind, optional style file, and optional import specifier.

`AppTopology.styles` and component `styles` rows are source-backed by local CSS imports and inline Aurelia `cssModules(...)` / `shadowCSS(...)` registry arguments. They distinguish plain component/global stylesheets from CSS module and Shadow DOM style registries. Use those rows for the `style-resource` expected effect and `style-resource-ownership` taste; dynamic class/style bindings are a separate template binding lane.
If generated fixtures fail verification, improve recognition/semantic-runtime proof layers rather than weakening topology into source-parser shortcuts.

anchors.summary: Shared relative authored import specifier helper for topology and source plans.
Application topology boundary and authoring pressure notes.

records.summary: Direct template bindings to DI-injected state and domain objects should remain visible without requiring view-model forwarding getters.
records.rationale: Idiomatic Aurelia can bind templates to ordinary DI-owned state and domain objects. A getter that only shortens `state.member` or forwards one domain property adds boilerplate and should not be required for AppTopology, observation, or authoring verification to see service/state flow.
guidance: Prefer `state.member` template bindings over view-model getters when the getter only forwards to DI-owned state.

Keep getters/setters for real adaptation points: route/query projection, presentation state, validation policy, service calls, or domain behavior. Nullable id-to-object lookup can be a real adaptation boundary, but generated form recipes currently express that boundary as a template-local `<let request.bind="state.readRequest(requestId)"></let>` plus `if.bind` narrowing so the component does not grow a forwarding getter.

ID-first boundaries and direct object bindings are both legitimate depending on the domain shape. Generated state-backed forms use a scalar ID at the component boundary, a template-local request object, and one object-valued `request.assignee` select with an app-authored matcher because the form model really owns that selected object.

Prefer domain-object getters for real domain behavior such as submit readiness. Generated form recipes model `ServiceRequest.canSubmit` as a plain accessor getter and bind to `request.canSubmit`/`canSubmit`; semantic-runtime should prove that through computed-observer-source rows and binding-observed-dependency rows instead of regenerating a view-model `canSubmit` alias.

When a binding source root is an injected state/service/model member, AppTopology.serviceInteractionBindings should publish direct read/write handoff rows from the binding data-flow instead of requiring a component-member TypeScript body.

Keep the direct handoff scope-backed: use the binding row's materialized BindingScope to prove that the root slot resolves to the injected component member source, so template locals named `state` do not become false service/state interactions.

Ground ordinary `state.member` observability in Aurelia expression connectable reads. ProxyObservable feeds watcher/trackable/nested-collection reads into the same observation circuit, but it is not a reason to reintroduce forwarding getters.

anchors.summary: Direct binding-data-flow to injected support-member topology rows.
Read/write operation projection from binding direction and assignment policy.
Application topology boundary and direct injected-state binding guidance.
Observation notes for ordinary template connectable reads and ProxyObservable as one integrated observation circuit.
Generated state-backed form recipe source plan using a ServiceRequest domain class and direct request.canSubmit template bindings.
Generated authoring fixture that binds request fields and request.canSubmit directly without view-model forwarding getters.
Hand-authored authoring fixture using direct nested checkout state bindings.
